title: IBS, Crohn's, and the Gut as Rage Storage
dek: *Your intestines remember every argument you swallowed, every boundary you didn't defend, every time you smiled when you wanted to scream. The gut doesn't forget—it just translates rage into a language of cramps, inflammation, and unpredictable urgency that conventional medicine often treats as purely mechanical.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Chronic gut conditions like IBS and Crohn's often correlate with suppressed rage that the nervous system stores in the digestive tract when fight-or-flight energy cannot discharge. The gut-brain axis translates unprocessed anger into inflammation, cramping, and motility changes through vagal tone shifts and stress hormone cascades. Releasing stored anger through somatic practices can reduce symptom severity in many cases, though medical supervision remains essential.

Anger isn't a problem to solve. It's information your body is trying to deliver, and when that delivery gets blocked at the level of expression, the message doesn't evaporate - it finds another route. For many people with IBS, Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, and other functional gut disorders, that alternate route runs straight through the digestive system. You've probably heard that stress affects the gut. What you might not have heard is that the specific flavor of stress matters, and suppressed anger creates a signature pattern in the enteric nervous system that looks clinically different from anxiety or grief. In my own practice, I've watched clients with decades of gut issues experience measurable relief once they learn to metabolize rage instead of storing it. This isn't about blaming yourself for being sick. It's about recognizing that your body is brilliantly, frustratingly logical - and that the same neural pathways that should carry anger outward can, when blocked, turn inward and rewrite the rules of digestion. ## The Gut-Brain Axis Runs on Unfinished Business Your gut contains roughly 500 million neurons - more than your spinal cord. This enteric nervous system communicates bidirectionally with your brain through the vagus nerve, a superhighway of information that carries far more signals upward (gut to brain) than downward. When you feel something "in your gut," you're detecting real neural activity, not metaphor. Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, has spent decades mapping how incomplete defensive responses stay lodged in the nervous system. He describes trauma as energy that wanted to become action - the fight or flight that never completed - and notes that this arrested energy doesn't just live in memory; it lives in tissue, in chronic muscle contraction, in the constant hum of a system that never got to finish what it started. Anger is the emotional signature of the fight response. When you're threatened, insulted, or violated, your sympathetic nervous system mobilizes energy to push back, set a boundary, or physically defend yourself. That mobilization involves specific changes: blood flow shifts toward large muscle groups, cortisol and adrenaline flood your system, your jaw tightens, your hands clench. If you complete the action - if you speak the truth, leave the room, or physically defend yourself - that energy discharges and your system returns to baseline. But what happens when you can't complete the action? When you're a child and fighting back means worse punishment? When you're an employee and speaking up means losing your income? When you're socialized as a woman and anger is coded as hysteria? The energy doesn't vanish. It redirects. And for many people, it redirects into the gut. Across the articles we've published on this site, we've seen a consistent pattern: people who report long histories of "swallowing" their anger also report gut symptoms that began or worsened during periods of high relational stress - bad marriages, toxic jobs, family estrangements. The correlation isn't coincidental. ## How Rage Rewrites Gut Function The NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system documents how chronic anger states alter vagal tone, shift the balance between sympathetic and parasympathetic activity, and create sustained elevations in inflammatory markers like IL-6 and TNF-alpha. These aren't abstract lab values - they're the biochemical signatures of a body stuck in defense mode. When anger can't discharge through action or expression, the sympathetic nervous system remains partially activated. This creates a cascade of effects in the gut: Your intestinal motility changes - either speeding up (diarrhea) or slowing down (constipation) as smooth muscle tension fluctuates. Blood flow to the digestive tract decreases because your body is prioritizing muscles for fight-or-flight. Gut permeability can increase, allowing partially digested food particles and bacteria to cross the intestinal barrier and trigger immune responses. The balance of your gut microbiome shifts as stress hormones alter the environment that beneficial bacteria need to thrive. In Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, where inflammation is the central feature, the link becomes even more direct. Chronic sympathetic activation keeps your immune system on high alert, and when the immune system is primed for threat, it can begin attacking the gut lining itself. This doesn't mean anger causes Crohn's in a simple linear way - genetics, microbiome composition, and environmental triggers all play roles - but suppressed rage can absolutely modulate disease activity and symptom severity. Pat Ogden, who developed Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, emphasizes that the body organizes experience differently than the conscious mind does. She notes that trauma and strong emotion create procedural memories - body-based patterns that bypass narrative understanding - and that these patterns continue to run until they're completed at the somatic level. You can understand intellectually why you're angry and still carry the incomplete muscular action of that anger in your gut wall, your diaphragm, your pelvic floor. ## The Physiology of Swallowing Rage Close your eyes for a moment and imagine someone saying something cruel to you. Notice what happens in your throat. For most people, there's a tightening, a closing - the physical act of swallowing words. That swallowing is literal. Your esophageal muscles contract, your larynx closes, and the energy that wanted to move up and out as speech gets pushed back down. Now notice what happens below your throat. Many people report a sensation of heat or pressure in the solar plexus, the upper abdomen, or deeper in the intestines. Some describe it as a clenching, a knot, a burning. This is the somatic reality of unexpressed anger finding a new home. Over years, that repeated pattern of swallowing becomes automatic. Your body learns that anger is dangerous, that expression leads to punishment or abandonment, and so it develops an efficient pathway: shut it down before it reaches your voice. The gut becomes a storage unit for everything you couldn't say. In our experience, people with IBS often describe their gut pain as having an emotional quality - not just physical cramping but a sense of trapped energy, frustration, or even wordless fury. When they learn to name that quality and give it conscious expression, the physical sensation often shifts or releases. ## Soraya Chemaly and the Gendered Gut Soraya Chemaly, in her work on women and anger, points out that girls are socialized from early childhood to suppress, deny, and redirect anger in ways that boys are not. She documents how this suppression correlates with higher rates of depression, anxiety, autoimmune conditions, and yes - gut disorders. Women are diagnosed with IBS at roughly twice the rate of men, and while hormonal factors play a role, the socialization piece is impossible to ignore. When you're taught that your anger is unattractive, unfeminine, irrational, or dangerous, you learn to bottle it. And the body, which cannot be gaslit, continues to generate the physiological response to injustice and violation. That response has to go somewhere. For many women, it goes into the gut, the pelvis, the throat - the soft, vulnerable places that hold what cannot be spoken. This isn't about gender essentialism. It's about recognizing that cultural conditioning shapes nervous system patterns, and that those patterns have measurable physical consequences. Men who are socialized to suppress vulnerability and express only anger can develop gut issues too, particularly when their anger itself becomes taboo in certain contexts - workplace hierarchies, caregiving roles, or relationships where emotional expression feels unsafe. The question isn't whether you have a right to be angry. The question is whether your nervous system believes it's safe to let that anger complete its cycle. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Releasing Gut-Stored Anger You don't need a therapist or a mountaintop to start discharging stored anger. You need privacy, permission, and a willingness to feel foolish. Find a space where you won't be interrupted. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent. Place one hand on your belly and one on your chest. Take three deep breaths, noticing any tightness or heat in your gut. Now, think of something that genuinely pisses you off - not the worst trauma of your life, just something irritating and unresolved. Notice where you feel it in your body. Most people will feel activation in the jaw, hands, belly, or shoulders. Begin to let sound come through. Not words yet - just sound. Growling, groaning, a low rumble from your belly. Let it be ugly. Let it be animal. As the sound comes, let your body move - pushing motions with your arms, stomping, twisting your torso. You're completing the action that got interrupted. Do this for two minutes. Then shake out your whole body for thirty seconds - loose, floppy, like a dog shaking off water. Finally, place both hands on your belly and take three slow breaths, noticing what's different. This isn't a cure. It's a single discharge cycle. But if you do this regularly - even a few times a week - you're teaching your nervous system that anger can move through and out instead of circling endlessly in your gut. If you want a deeper framework for this kind of work, [Anger: Wisdom for Cooling the Flames - Thich Nhat Hanh](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1594484473?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a contemplative approach that honors both the validity of anger and the need to metabolize it skillfully. For a more body-centered clinical method, [Sensorimotor Psychotherapy - Pat Ogden](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0393708810?tag=spankyspinola-20) lays out the neuroscience and practice steps in detail. ## When the Gut Needs Both Medicine and Movement Here's what this approach is not: a replacement for medical care. If you have Crohn's disease, you need gastroenterology. If you're bleeding, losing weight, or experiencing severe pain, you need diagnostics and possibly medication. Somatic work with anger is an adjunct, not an alternative. But medication alone often leaves people only partially better. Biologics can reduce inflammation, antispasmodics can calm cramping, but if the underlying nervous system pattern continues to generate stress signals, symptoms often persist or cycle. Over the years I've seen clients on every medication combination imaginable still struggling with flares, and the common thread is almost always unmetabolized relational stress. The best outcomes happen when you address both levels: the biochemical inflammation and the nervous system pattern feeding it. That might mean a biologic plus somatic therapy. Or a low-FODMAP diet plus anger work. Or a probiotic plus boundary-setting practice. Some practitioners recommend herbs that support both liver and nervous system function during anger work. [Planetary Herbals Bupleurum Liver Cleanse](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0002YFGBM?tag=spankyspinola-20) is one option that combines traditional Chinese medicine principles with gut support, though as always, check with your doctor before adding supplements to your regimen, especially if you're on immunosuppressants. The point is integration. Your gut is not separate from your nervous system, your nervous system is not separate from your relational history, and your relational history is not separate from the cultural water you've been swimming in your whole life. Healing happens when you stop treating the gut as a mechanical problem and start treating it as the exquisitely sensitive emotional organ it actually is. ## The Body Keeps the Score, and the Gut Keeps the Receipts Your gut is not betraying you. It's doing exactly what it was designed to do - respond to threat, store incomplete responses, and signal when something is unfinished. The cramping, the urgency, the inflammation - these are messages, not malfunctions. When you begin to listen to those messages instead of just suppressing symptoms, a different kind of healing becomes possible. Not always a cure - some gut damage is irreversible, some genetic factors are immutable - but a shift in your relationship to your own body. A recognition that your gut has been trying to tell you something important, and that learning its language might be the missing piece. You can explore more tools for working with anger somatically in [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit), which includes practices for everything from rage release to boundary repair. And if you're curious about the broader philosophy behind this work, [the about page](/about) explains how we approach the intersection of emotion, embodiment, and healing. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Can suppressing anger actually cause Crohn's disease or IBS in someone who didn't have it before? It's more accurate to say that suppressed anger can be a contributing factor in a complex web of genetic predisposition, microbiome composition, early life stress, and immune function. Most research suggests that gut disorders are multifactorial - no single cause creates them. But chronic sympathetic activation from unprocessed anger can absolutely tip a vulnerable system into active disease, worsen existing inflammation, and increase the frequency and severity of flares. You're not causing your illness by having emotions; you're just dealing with a body that's trying to process those emotions through the only channels it has available. ### What if I've done tons of therapy and talked about my anger but my gut symptoms haven't changed? Talk therapy accesses the narrative, cognitive parts of your brain - the parts that can explain and analyze. But anger stored in the gut lives in the procedural, body-based memory systems that don't respond to talking alone. You need somatic discharge - actual physical completion of the defensive movements your body wanted to make but couldn't. This is why approaches like Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or even trauma-informed yoga can create shifts that years of talk therapy didn't touch. The body needs to move the energy through, not just understand where it came from. For more context on this distinction, you might find [more articles in this journal](/articles) helpful, particularly pieces on embodied processing. ### Is this approach saying that gut disease is "all in my head" or that I'm imagining my symptoms? Absolutely not. Your symptoms are real, your inflammation is measurable, your pain is valid. What we're saying is that the gut-brain axis is bidirectional and that psychological stress - particularly suppressed anger - creates real, physical changes in gut function and immune activity. This isn't psychosomatic in the dismissive sense; it's psycho-neuro-immuno-gastroenterological. Your thoughts, emotions, nervous system, immune system, and gut are one integrated system, not separate kingdoms. Addressing the emotional component doesn't mean the physical component isn't real - it means you're treating the whole system instead of just one piece of it. ## The Conversation Your Gut Wants to Have Somewhere in your body right now, there's a conversation that never finished. A boundary that needed defending. A truth that wanted speaking. A No that got swallowed. Your gut has been holding that unfinished business, waiting for the moment when it becomes safe enough to complete. That moment might be now. Not because your circumstances are perfect or your relationships are healed, but because you're finally ready to listen to what your body has been trying to say. The anger isn't the enemy. The suppression is. And learning to let anger move through you - cleanly, completely, without harming yourself or others - might be the most radical act of self-care available. This work isn't quick and it isn't always comfortable, but it's honest in a way that treating symptoms alone can never be. Your gut is asking for more than medication. It's asking for permission to feel, to speak, to push back against what should never have been acceptable in the first place. And when you give that permission, when you finally let the stored rage discharge, you might find that your gut has been waiting for this moment all along. For related perspectives on anger as a sacred messenger rather than a problem to fix, [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) explores the devotional dimensions of working with difficult emotions. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on ibs, crohn's, and the gut as rage storage, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
